Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd try to set up a schedule at the beginning of each week, but then life would hit me and everything would fall apart. I'd end up cramming late at night, watching lecture videos at 2am, trying to make sense of everything before deadlines. I spent about 21 hours a week studying, but it felt chaotic and exhausting rather than productive. I always had good intentions, but executing them was another story entirely.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials because I was dealing with extracurricular commitments or just feeling too anxious to focus. Then when deadlines approached, I'd panic and try to do everything at once. I wish I could say I had regular study times, but the truth is I was constantly playing catch-up and it wore me down.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or realize I'd missed something important, and that fear would push me onto the LMS. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to check in. It wasn't really a proactive thing for me—more reactive, driven by anxiety about falling behind.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, especially after connecting with some peers in the course who'd remind me about things. But those phases never lasted long. The anxiety of seeing all the things I hadn't done made me want to avoid it, which I know sounds counterproductive.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really try to understand concepts at my own pace. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students would explain things in simpler terms than the professor. I watched over 100 minutes of video content, and those were the moments I felt like I was actually learning something.
Q6: I often ignored the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice exercises. When you're already overwhelmed and barely keeping up with required work, optional stuff just feels like extra weight you can't carry. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion posts I made were mostly when I really needed help, not for regular participation.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or understanding something I was struggling with. If it helped me prepare for an exam or complete an assignment, I'd prioritize it. But if something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to survive the semester, I'd push it aside. Time felt so precious and scarce that I had to be ruthless about what I spent it on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work already had me stretched thin, so optional stuff just made me feel guilty for not doing it. There was always this voice in my head saying I should do more, but my body and mind were already exhausted. Required activities came with the pressure of grades, while optional ones just added to my sense of inadequacy when I couldn't get to them.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet without being connected. I'd make handwritten notes because writing things out helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts better than our course materials. None of that would show up in any system tracking.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours or working through problems on paper, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person—we'd quiz each other, explain concepts, work through confusion together. That peer support was actually really valuable for my learning, but it was completely invisible to any online tracking.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice if I was really confused, but then I'd work from my notes and downloaded materials for days afterward. The initial access was just the starting point. Most of my actual learning happened in the repeated review of those materials offline, sitting in the library or at home with my notebook.
Q12: There were several weeks where everything just felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, and I'd feel paralyzed—unable to start anything because the mountain of work felt insurmountable. I was dealing with some personal stuff and my extracurricular commitments were demanding. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like too much because I knew I'd see all the things I was failing at.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. I'd click through materials but nothing would stick. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and the sheer cognitive load of managing three online courses meant I was often running on empty. The LMS became something I dreaded rather than a helpful tool.
Q14: Many times. I'd open readings or start videos but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other assignments, feeling anxious about my performance, or just mentally checked out from exhaustion. I'd technically be 'engaging' with the material, but I wasn't really there. It was like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The bigger or more important something felt, the more anxious it made me, and that anxiety led to avoidance. It's this terrible cycle where you know avoiding makes things worse, but you can't seem to break out of it. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel even more behind.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I'd drift and procrastinate endlessly. But they also caused intense stress—I'd cram everything into the last possible hours, which meant the quality of my work suffered. My engagement with the LMS would spike right before due dates and then drop off completely afterward. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it gave me a temporary boost of motivation. But negative feedback or poor grades would send me into a spiral of self-doubt that made it even harder to engage. The peer support intervention helped because getting encouragement from classmates felt less judgmental than official feedback. It reminded me I wasn't alone in struggling.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. Having multiple components to track across three online courses meant I was constantly confused about what was due when. The structure assumed a level of organization and self-discipline that I was struggling to maintain. When things were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines, I did better, but ambiguity made my anxiety worse.
Q19: The video content being available to watch anytime was helpful for my visual learning style. But the sheer amount of material made engagement harder—there was always more to do than I could manage. Having discussion forums where I could ask questions helped, but I often felt too embarrassed to post because I thought my questions were too basic. Clearer guidance on priorities would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. You're clicking around, opening things, trying to figure out what you need to do, but you're too overwhelmed or confused to actually produce anything. High activity can mean you're lost and searching rather than efficiently working. Or you're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are just more efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it, and work offline. Or they have other resources like textbooks or study groups that don't require LMS time. I think I fell somewhere in between—my LMS activity was low but my actual studying was higher, though still not as effective as it could have been because of how scattered I was.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see if you're actually understanding material or just staring at it blankly. They miss offline studying, peer collaboration, the emotional state you're in while engaging. A log might show I watched a video, but not that I was crying from stress while watching it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just going through motions to pass. It means feeling curious, asking questions, wanting to understand rather than just survive. For me, true engagement happened in rare moments when my anxiety quieted down enough to actually think. It's a mental and emotional state, not just a behavior that can be tracked.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It can't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, the tears of frustration, the genuine desire to do better that kept getting crushed by overwhelm. It can't show the peer support that kept me going—the texts from classmates, the study sessions, the emotional encouragement. My struggle and my effort are invisible in the data.
